She said it would have been best to retain more of the existing, look at VEing options, etc. She noted that they had to comply with HMO requirements for this job (as seems to be the case for all their jobs) and noted that Optivo probably don't need to do this because they are a Housing Association (we should have known that). She wanted to see more projects to show that we get the space requirements for student resi (dig out Folkestone). She is working on a job in Richmond that is at feasibility stage. In the summer they will refurbish 2 properties. They have a 10 year old portfolio and they are about to refurbish a lot of it. Just sold 4 buildings so have capital to invest. They will start by refurbishing 40 rooms in 2 cities as a test and, if profitable, will roll this out to the rest of their portfolio. She said they are a young company like us and that they give a bespoke service. Buildings are max. 50-200 rooms. They want to have more city presence. Their preference is to have 200 rooms across 3 building instead of a large building with 200 rooms (as an example). They target international market, and understand they appeal to these students (city centre location, 10-20 min to university). Often they do quite a traditional FF&E. Preference is more stud

contacts.name: Aaron Tomsett
contacts.notes:
[FIRST MET]
messaged him on Linkedin.

[NOTES]
On the call I made up that I heard from him probably through Joe Haire and a mutual connection at Morgan Capital. He was working in Australia as a QS for Costplan and he suggested to them a 5 year plan where he was going to start the UK office. He has done that and has a 75% stake, the other 25% is James Mitchell. There were 3 original founders in Australia, one of them retired and 2 are about to retire. There are 4 Directors in Australia at the moment. The UK office is in Ashford and he knew our project there. They also have another office near Cambridge that they have just set up. His role is moving more towards CEO as they are creating an umbrella group. He has a 25% in the Australia office and another 25% in the Papua one. Papua's market is very busy and they just secured a £1.5M fee job for the next 3 years. They also offer planning services but do QS mainly. They do loads of resi schemes. For Morgan Capital they act as Employer Agents and tend to be appointed a few months before anything happens. Sometimes they are asked to recommend architects and they are happy to try to make an intro. They don't want to grow to more than 10-15 people per office. Aaron said I should meet his co-director James Mitchell to see how we can help each other. UPDATE coffee at WR office on 02/09/2021 Aaron and James came and met DL and JJ. Aaron seems more focused on public work and working for resi contractors. James is the connection to Morgan Capital, sounded like probably Mark Hellawell is his contact there. They are doing a couple of things with them and doesn't mind making an intro. He said that at 16-17 Hanover Street Morgan Capital approached the building owner to be DM and that the building owner paid too much for it. Mentioned that they could help with a 'project in Maidstone' (the PowerHub, didn't mention the name). They both have two children each (a boy and a gilr) and both had kids starting school this week.

contacts.name: Peter Mahoney
contacts.notes:
[FIRST MET]
connected randomly on Linkedin and had a call on 23/03/2021.

[NOTES]
connected randomly on Linkedin and had a call on 23/03/2021. Really nice guy, long chat. He was at Rogers in the golden era (end of 90s) and enjoyed his time there but thought that his skills were more aligned with being client. They are also delivering a shell & core office space but seems a one-off. They focus in mix use resi, interesting projects. Their latest project includes an incubator area to create community (www.makerworks.co.uk) but it has been challenging. They look both for industrial and commercial land in the outer boroughs. Normally get planning themselves and then sell this to a housing association, then they buy it back to manage it? Could this be a way to avoid the affordability thresholds? However this model takes too long now. Has looked at modular for a long time but doesn't think it works. However he likes the idea of LGS and screw piling. One of their latest projects is a PRS scheme in Brent, 251 units funded by a pension fund. Thinks that PRS backed by a fund is the only thing that seems to work now and that everybody will follow the trend. Said that in the UK typically follow what works and try to make it better. Said we should definitely grab a beer soon and should stay in touch. Likes good architecture and working with good architects. Met him for beers on 20/10/21 They have resumed operations a few weeks ago. Ex-AA, did great things at his unit like getting ‘millenium funding’ including going to the house of lords for it. Lover putting things together so the AA was not the right fit. He had an 8-person inhouse team of architects until 18months ago, now trying to keep it lean. Loves our enterprenurial side, keen to come to the office in 2 weeks (away for half term to a house he has in France) and meet Joe and Dicky. He will share contact for the branding guy they use. Share Bradley August Notion thing, he loved it. He is pitching for a £60M cash project

[FIRST MET]
DL met her around 2019 and have kind of followed up with her. JH and JJ had also briefly met her

[NOTES]
JJ coffee on 15/06/21. Jess came to WR new office and loved it. They are very busy. Seem to be delivering very well to a set of specific clients and get a lot of repeated work. She said it is still kind of secret but she is splitting from her business partner Lucy. Apparently they had some issues pre-pandemic but when covid hit she decided to move to Brighton and has decided not to come back to London. Also they want to be doing different things. Now they are still agreeing on the sum for Jess to buy Lucy's part of the business. Want to grow organically to around 15 - they are 5 now and looking to hire -. She says offsetting carbon footprints is bullshit and there is a lot of greenwashing around that. She thinks that circular economy is key but that there can be a lot of bullshit if not done right. She loved the fact that we are building a materials/ suppliers library and was very interested in the carpet guys that do Net Zero (check company name and send it to her) She said Aitch Group are very busy and they are working on all their projects. They started with them many years ago when doing a 7 flat refurbishment and now they are doing 300-400 schemes with them. She said that they tend to use big-ish architects but that they tend to use new architects all the time. They have used Formation Architects, BGY, etc. (and apparently fell out with BGY so not using them anymore). She thinks quite a few architects they use are not good, not good designers. She offered to set up drinks with Aitch Group (Nick and Luke). She said they are mates and meet up and have drinks often. Loved the idea of having the drinks outside WR and try our brew. UPDATE 03/09/21 called to see if she could help with Vile Parle and she said yes and that we should get together for a coffee. They have just hired 3 people and they are crazy busy. She is in a conference all week next wee

contacts.name: Robert Skioldebrand
contacts.position: Director - Central London Office Agency & Development
contacts.notes:
[FIRST MET]
JH Cold emailed on 13/11/2023 suggesting a call later on the week

[MEETING PREP]
Rob joined Bray Fox Smith in 2019 after 10 years Montagu Evans. Rob is a senior, experienced Central London office agent with a proven track record and strong client following. He has advised private investors, developers, landed estates, institutions and tenants in all aspects of commercial office space and mixed-use property. This includes major office leasing assignments; long-term development consultancy projects; development site sales and acquisition; and corporate occupier representation focusing on acquisition, disposals, lease restructuring, consolidation, cost cutting and turn-key solutions. His goal is to grow the scale and capability of the Central London agency offer at BFS, working alongside the other market leading decentralised experts already in the firm. Rob’s property career spans 30 years, with his Agency life starting at Strutt & Parker, before moving on to Weatherall Green & Smith, Jones Lang LaSalle and penultimately Montagu Evans, where he helped build and diversify the agency only teams into efficient multi discipline sector groups. Rob was a Regional Director at JLL before joining Montagu Evans as a full profit sharing partner in 2009. Born in Stockholm, Sweden, but has since spent most of his formative years in England, living in Surrey, where he went to Kingston Grammar School, and then studied Estate Management in Southampton. Rob lives in Notting Hill and has a daughter and a son who are at Bristol University and Winchester College respectively. Rob regularly used to play hockey for Surrey county, athletics for the Borough of Kingston, row for University, and ski since he could walk. Since his sporting heyday, he has since competed in several triathlons and long distance swimming events, and now enjoys restoring classic cars, classic boats and country cottages. He also has a habit of collecting interesting vintage “stuff”.

[NOTES]
Really cool asset manager/ developer. Got the details to make an intro to them from James Morgan (CHP) on an email on 28/04/21. Theo Michel and Patrick O'Gorman came to our office on 11/05/21 to meet DL and JJ. Theo and Richard Walker (owner of Iceland) started it after they met as graduates at JLL. Patrick joined later and also invests in the projects (met when he was at CBRE Poland). Patrick is a good communicator and seems to be the people's person. Theo seemed quite serious at the start and more relaxed later on. Not an easy start as they had been told by James Morgan that we had something more specific. They are all about sustainability and said that they like being transparent about what they know and don't know. Their investors and tenants are all the time asking them difficult questions about sustainability. They understand that it is a long road and they are here for the long run. They hate green-washing. They are looking for commercial/ office spaces in central London. From £50M-£200M and are keen to see how buildings can be refreshed. They are working with Feilden Clegg Bradley but sometimes they get a partner to pitch and then they deal with an associate they don't like. They like dealing with them in Belfast because they are a team of 12 and a great associate runs it. They are seeing that at their Paradise building Feilden Clegg Bradley and Buro Happold are overdoing certain stages and feel that they don't seem to understand that often is best to deal with certain things after planning to keep costs down.
